summary: Gruber challenges a Bloomberg report by Mark Gurman claiming Apple is aggressively pushing employees back to offices sooner than its tech rivals. After speaking with multiple Apple sources, Gruber argues the story's narrative is bullshit — the facts buried in the piece only show that Apple is bringing back employees who can't work remotely, which is no different from any other company. He contends that Apple's leadership is actively encouraging work-from-home and that Apple Park remains a 'ghost town,' with no real timeline for broader return. Gruber also ties this to Bloomberg's broader credibility problems stemming from the unretracted 'Big Hack' story.
key_points: Bloomberg's headline frames Apple as uniquely aggressive about office returns, but the article's own facts only describe bringing back employees who can't work remotely — identical to every other tech company
Gruber's Apple sources say there is no real timeline for phases 2 or 3, managers face no pressure to bring teams back, and leadership is 'totally fine' with employees not returning anytime soon
Apple's hardware focus means more employees genuinely need on-site access to physical tools, which is an obvious distinction from software-focused rivals, not evidence of cultural aggression
Bloomberg's report contains zero on-the-record quotes and frames a collaborative manager-employee conversation process as top-down directives
Gruber uses the piece to remind readers of Bloomberg's unretracted 'Big Hack' story, arguing their institutional credibility remains damaged
money_quotes: I call bullshit. This narrative conveys the opposite of what's going on at Apple.

There's a difference between outright falsehoods and bullshit. The above can all be true — I have one specific point to dispute, which I'll get to below — but still be bullshit in the context of the narrative gist of the story.
There is no real timeline associated with phases 2 or 3 yet.

There are no plans for Apple Park to return to anything but 'a goddamn ghost town' — one source's words — until the COVID-19 outbreak is under control, perhaps not until there is a vaccine or therapeutic treatments with proven efficacy.

Apple Is Approaching Return to Office Work Cautiously and Humanely, Just Like Other Companies in Tech, Albeit With the Added but Obvious Burden of Having More Employees Who Work on Hardware isn't much of a headline, though.
Bloomberg's institutional credibility is severely damaged, and everything they publish should be treated with skepticism until they retract the story or provide evidence that it was true.
key_insight: Bloomberg's report manufactures a misleading narrative about Apple's return-to-office plans by framing ordinary, hardware-necessitated arrangements as culturally aggressive — a distinction that exists only in the headline, not in the facts.
